title: June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated | Stats NZ
content:
## June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
11 September 2020, 2:30pm
This page outlines the data sources that are being used to compile and analyse Gross domestic product: June 2020 quarter, which will be released on 17 September 2020. This updates information published on 28 August 2020 about the measurement challenges that we are dealing with due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19.
Note: The information provided in Summary of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components is accurate at this point in time. Further updates will be provided in the release of Gross domestic product: June 2020 quarter , to be released on 17 September 2020.
- Methodological challenges
- Output indicators: Relationships between gross output and intermediate consumption
- Employment indicators
- Alternative data sources that will be used for Gross domestic product: June 2020
- Summary of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components

## Methodological challenges
Details about methodological challenges were previously published in COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters and June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19 .
The following papers have also been released covering measuring COVID-19 in June 2020 quarter for releases. These releases are input data sources into the compilation of GDP.
- COVID-19 lockdown and value of building work put in place for June 2020 quarter
- Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter
- COVID-19 and labour market statistics in the June 2020 quarter
- Impacts of COVID-19 on the methodology used for the June 2020 quarter CPI

## Output indicators: Relationships between gross output and intermediate consumption
Most sub-components of GDP production use quarterly output indicators as a proxy for the change in value added. The underlying assumption of stable relationships between gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added can change following abrupt shifts in activity, such as those caused by the alert level 4 lockdown.
For example, a business heavily reliant on tourism may have fixed costs remaining near to usual levels but experience a sharp drop in sales.
If we use the volume of sales as our quarterly indicator, we will not capture the impact of the stable costs, which implies a larger decline in value added. If this effect is significant and not corrected for, growth will be overstated in the quarter. This may also lead to revisions in the future when more complete and accurate estimates of value added become available.

## Employment indicators
Paid hours data sourced from the quarterly employment survey (QES) (released in Labour market statistics ) are used as a quarterly indicator for a range of components in GDP(P) and GDP(E). The use of this data source has two main limitations in the June 2020 quarter:
- It is a mid-quarter estimate, and so for June 2020 quarter will represent pay periods ending on or immediately before 20 May 2020. How representative this is of the whole quarter given the different COVID-19 alert levels in place is questionable.
- QES paid hours data may deviate substantially from hours worked due to the COVID-19 wage subsidy, and so will not be a suitable proxy for activity in the June 2020 quarter.
Quarterly employment survey – DataInfo+ has information about the methodology used in this survey.
Gross earnings data sourced from the Employer Monthly Schedule (EMS) is also used in some components. This data source will also include the wage subsidy and so is not a suitable proxy for activity in the June 2020 quarter.
Employment-based indicators are weaker proxies for value added when compared to other data sources. COVID-19-related measurement challenges have brought forward planned work to review the use of QES indicators and explore updating methodologies to use data sources such as the Business data collection (see below) in the measurement of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. Any permanent methods changes will be implemented in September 2020 quarter, in line with our standard timing for incorporating new methodologies and revisions to our timeseries.

## Alternative data sources that will be used in Gross domestic product: June 2020
The following data sources have been utilised for confrontation and, where deemed necessary, have replaced usual methods where these have been unable to appropriately capture activity in June 2020 quarter.

### Business data collection
The Business data collection (BDC) uses administrative data and financial data directly collected from businesses to produce measures of sales, purchases, salaries and wages, and operating profit.

#### Identifying divergences in gross output and intermediate consumption
For sub-industry output indicators on the production side of GDP, we used BDC sales and purchases data to identify significant changes in the relationships between gross output and intermediate consumption.
BDC sales data deflated by the corresponding output price deflator was used to represent the volume of gross output, while BDC purchases data deflated by the corresponding input price index was used to represent the volume of intermediate consumption. Data was adjusted where appropriate for changes in stock levels, and seasonally adjusted. We used this to calculate gross output to intermediate consumption ratios for each subindustry.
If the June 2020 quarter ratio was more than two standard deviations away from the rolling average ratio derived from the full BDC timeseries, the component was flagged as showing a significant divergence in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption.
For components where the usual method was unable to adequately represent activity in the June 2020 quarter and displayed a normal gross output to intermediate consumption ratio, we used the volume of BDC sales instead of the usual method.
For components where the usual method was unable to adequately represent activity in the June 2020 quarter and was flagged as not displaying a normal gross output to intermediate consumption ratio, BDC value added in volume terms was used instead of the usual method. Derived value added was calculated as sales less purchases (in volume terms) for most industries. For industries where we had stocks data, the value added was calculated as sales less purchases plus change in finished goods stocks plus change in raw material stocks, in volume terms.
In both these cases, the quarterly percentage change for June 2020 quarter was applied to the series.
In applying the BDC data, we accounted for differences in seasonality between the usual method and the BDC data.
There were a few cases where the derived BDC value added data for June 2020 quarter was not fit for purpose, and so, despite the gross output to intermediate consumption ratio indicating a change in the relationship, we did not have sufficient data to account for it.
We also used BDC sales data in some components of GDP(E), in cases where the usual approach would not appropriately capture expenditure. We used current price BDC sales data for components that are usually compiled in current prices, and BDC sales volumes data for components usually compiled in volume terms.
The Household labour force survey (released in Labour market statistics ) collects actual and usual hours worked. We are using data by week and sub-industry to understand activity throughout the June 2020 quarter.
Household labour force survey – DataInfo+ has information about the general methodology used for this survey.
Employment indicators (EI) uses administrative data to produce monthly measures of filled jobs and gross earnings.
Employment indicators – DataInfo + provides general methodology and definitions relevant for employment statistics.
Low-level filled jobs data is being used to understand changes in employment during the June 2020 quarter.

#### Household labour force survey/ Employment indicators composite
We combined the two sources of employment data – household labour force survey (HLFS) and employment indicators EI) – to create a composite indicator, for confrontation against other data sources and, in limited cases, to replace usual methods for the June 2020 quarter.
The composite measure is a quarterly estimate of activity, calculated by multiplying the number of filled jobs from the EI, by the proportion of usual hours that were actually worked from the HLFS. This was compiled at a subindustry level and aggregated up from low level HFLS data which was collected on a weekly basis.
We compared estimated activity from the HLFS/EI indicator over the June 2020 quarter against a March 2020 quarter baseline. The movement was confronted against our usual quarterly indicators and the BDC to support our understanding of activity over the June 2020 quarter.
This composite indicator addressed two challenges with our normal use of QES data:
- It covers the full quarter, by using the EI and HLFS; and
- We have a measure of actual hours worked.
There are caveats around our use of this data:
- The HLFS actual hours worked data does have variable sample sizes at a subindustry level, and these were taken into consideration when looking at low level data.
- We have assumed that there is no change in the usual hours per worker within a subindustry, as we consider variation in this from the HLFS data to be more influenced by changes in the composition of the sample.
Overall, the HLFS/EI composite indicator aligned well with other data sources and showed reasonable stability from week to week within alert levels. As such, we deemed it suitable for use to understand the level of activity through the quarter.
We implemented the HLFS/EI composite indicator as our June 2020 quarter measure of activity in a handful of cases. This was used when we did not have another reliable source of quarterly data, and there was evidence to suggest that activity in that particular component was impacted by the changes in alert levels. The majority of components that used this data were low level with small weights and therefore had minimal impacts to higher level published components.

### Electronic card transactions
We have access to electronic card transactions (ECT) data. This data will be used to understand the level of activity through the June 2020 quarter.

### Areas where we lack a quarterly indicator
Interpolation of the annual benchmark without a quarterly indicator is used for some lower-level components in GDP(P) and GDP(E). This method is generally only used for smaller components. It assumes little variation in the quarterly change from historical trends and is unable to capture sharp shifts in activity.
We have reviewed all components that use this method and will confront and where appropriate will use alternative data sources (BDC, HLFS, EI, ECT data as well as other industry-specific data sources) to estimate these components.
More detail about the use of alternative data sources instead of usual quarterly approaches is provided below in the following section.

## Summary of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
Below we note the components of GDP production and GDP expenditure where we have deviated from the usual quarterly indicators and/or methods, and the data sources we will be using for this. Please note that the information below is valid as at date of release and is subject to change.
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Metal ore and non-metallic mineral mining and quarrying normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived value-added from the BDC will be used for the June 2020 quarter.
We normally use output indicators sourced from the economic survey of manufacturing to measure activity. The following subindustries will use BDC value added (including change in stock levels) to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption in June 2020 quarter:
- textile, leather, clothing and footwear manufacturing
- furniture and other manufacturing.

#### Electricity, gas, water, and waste services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Waste collection, treatment, and disposal normally uses QES. We will use BDC sales volumes for the June 2020 quarter.
Output indicators are normally sourced from value of building work put in place and are directly collected for private-sector heavy and civil construction.
Changes for the components of this industry:
- QES indicator is normally used for the public-sector component of heavy and civil engineering construction, while the private sector component uses the movement in the ‘Other construction’ category of gross fixed capital formation (GFKF). Derived value-added volumes from the BDC will be used due to changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption in June 2020 quarter.
- Construction services is normally derived from the combined movement in residential and non-residential building construction, which is sourced from ‘value of building work done’ data. BDC data indicates issues in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios, and so derived BDC valued added will be used in June 2020 quarter.

#### Retail trade, accommodation, and food services
All subindustries in this industry normally use output indicators sourced from retail trade survey data. The following subindustries will use derived BDC value-added (including change in stock levels) to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption in June 2020 quarter:
- specialised food retailing
- recreational, clothing, footwear and personal accessory retailing
- food and beverage services
- accommodation services.

#### Transport, postal, and warehousing services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Air and space transport normally uses passenger and freight revenue kilometres, collected directly from major companies. In June 2020 quarter we will use derived BDC value-added volumes to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption.
- Scenic and sightseeing transport normally uses international visitor numbers. In June 2020 quarter we will use BDC sales volumes.
- Other transport normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived BDC value-added volumes will be used to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption.
- Warehousing and storage services normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In June 2020 quarter we will use BDC sales volumes.

#### Information, media and telecommunications
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Publishing (except internet and music publishing) normally uses QES. BDC value added will be used in the June 2020 quarter.
- Motion picture and sound recording activities normally uses gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule, which will overstate work done due to the impact of the wage subsidy. HLFS/EI composite movement will be used to reflect activity in June 2020 quarter.
- Broadcasting and internet publishing normally uses gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule, which will overstate work done due to the impact of the wage subsidy. Derived value added from the BDC will be used for June 2020 quarter.
- Library and other information services does not have a quarterly indicator. Working day adjustments will be used for the June 2020 quarter, which assumes that the library component of this subindustry did not operate under levels 3 and 4.

#### Rental, hiring, and real estate services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- A low-level component of this industry – rental and hiring services (except real estate) – has a quarterly methodology of interpolation of the annual with no quarterly indicator. ECT data was used to estimate activity for the March 2020 quarter. For June 2020 quarter, BDC sales volumes will be used.

#### Professional, scientific, technical, administrative, and support services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Material changes to gross output to intermediate consumption ratios in the June 2020 quarter were identified in the following sub-industries: Scientific, architectural and engineering services; Advertising , market research, and management services; Legal and accounting services; Veterinary and other professional services; Travel agency and tour arrangement services; and Employment and other administrative services. All these sub-industries will use value-added movements derived from BDC data in June 2020 quarter.
- Advertising, market research, and management services normally uses QES indicators. The derived BDC value added will be used for the June 2020 quarter. From September 2020 quarter we anticipate a permanent change to using BDC sales volumes data.
- Computer system design and related services uses QES indicators. The BDC sales volumes will replace the QES indicator in the March and June 2020 quarters. We anticipate changing to the use of BDC sales volumes data permanently from the September 2020 quarter.
- Travel agency and tour arrangement services normally use QES indicators. In March 2020 quarter GST sales data was used to represent the change in activity. The derived BDC value-added will be used for the June 2020 quarter.
- Building, cleaning, pest control, and other support services normally use QES indicators. BDC sales volumes will be used in the June 2020 quarter.

#### Education and training
All education and training sub industries normally use a method of interpolation of the annual without a quarterly indicator. The following approaches will be used to represent activity in this industry:
- Preschool education was estimated in March 2020 quarter using an assumption of no activity for the days of the alert level 4 lockdown, and 100 percent activity at alert level 1. For the June 2020 quarter we will use cumulative attendance hours.
- The method for school education will not be changed. Activity is assumed to be unchanged, with remote learning assumed to be a direct substitute for face-to-face learning.
- Tertiary education was estimated using student visa data in March 2020 quarter and will be again in June 2020 quarter.
- Adult, community, and other education was adjusted using unit-level information to estimate activity during alert level 4. In June 2020 quarter BDC sales will be used.

#### Healthcare and social assistance
The following sub-industries normally use QES indicators, and will use alternative indicators for March and June 2020 quarters:
- The market component of hospital services used BDC sales volumes in March 2020 quarter and this will be used again in the June 2020 quarter.
- The market component of Private medical and other health care services used BDC sales volumes in March 2020 quarter and will use a HLFS/EI composite indicator in June 2020 quarter.
- Residential care services and social assistance will use BDC sales volumes in June 2020 quarter.

#### Arts, recreation, and other services
The following sub-industries normally use QES indicators, but will use other sources as stated for the March and June 2020 quarters:
- Heritage and artistic activities will use BDC sales volumes
- Sport and recreation activities will use BDC sales volumes
- Gambling activities will use BDC sales volumes
- Religious services will use HLFS/EI composite indicator for June 2020 quarter
- Civil, professional, and other interest groups will use derived value added from BDC.
Note that in cases where changes have been made to line up to corresponding components on the production side of GDP, the different underlying concepts between measurement of production and expenditure have been accounted for.

#### Non-profit organisations serving households
- Interpolation without an indicator is the method used for this component
- A working day adjustment will be made to March and June 2020 quarters, with the assumption of reduced activity at alert levels 3 and 4.
- Other household services were adjusted in March and June 2020 quarter to account for days lost due to the alert level 4 lockdown.
- Other private transport costs used weighted movements from sub-industries in transport in March 2020 quarter. In June 2020 quarter, low level BDC sales values will be used.
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using ECT data in March 2020 quarter. In June 2020 quarter low level BDC sales values will be used.
- Recreational services, and cultural services both normally use QES indicators, in line with heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in the arts and recreation industry. These subindustries will use BDC sales values in March and June 2020 quarters.
- Hospital services and out-patient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance, using BDC sales values.
- For HCE on education, domestic expenditure was aligned with the approaches used for GDP(P) education sub-industries in March and June 2020 quarters. The measurement of education travel credits is consistent with the approach used in our balance of payments.
- Veterinary services normally uses counts of practicing vets. In June 2020 quarter we will use low level BDC sales values.
- A component of miscellaneous services will use BDC sales values data.
- Maintenance materials and maintenance services normally are imputed using the higher class level. In June 2020 quarter we will use retail trade data for maintenance materials, and maintenance services will use low-level BDC sales values data.
- The price index used in the compilation of HCE rents incorporates the rental price freeze.

#### Household consumption expenditure
- Other household services were adjusted in March and June 2020 quarter to account for days lost due to the alert level 4 lockdown.
- Other private transport costs used weighted movements from sub-industries in transport in March 2020 quarter. In June 2020 quarter, low level BDC sales values will be used.
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using ECT data in March 2020 quarter. In June 2020 quarter low level BDC sales values will be used.
- Recreational services, and cultural services both normally use QES indicators, in line with heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in the arts and recreation industry. These subindustries will use BDC sales values in March and June 2020 quarters.
- Hospital services and out-patient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance, using BDC sales values.
- For HCE on education, domestic expenditure was aligned with the approaches used for GDP(P) education sub-industries in March and June 2020 quarters. The measurement of education travel credits is consistent with the approach used in our balance of payments.
- Veterinary services normally uses counts of practicing vets. In June 2020 quarter we will use low level BDC sales values.
- A component of miscellaneous services will use BDC sales values data.
- Maintenance materials and maintenance services normally are imputed using the higher class level. In June 2020 quarter we will use retail trade data for maintenance materials, and maintenance services will use low-level BDC sales values data.
- The price index used in the compilation of HCE rents incorporates the rental price freeze.
HCE is calculated by deducting net tourist spending from total domestic expenditure, for low level HCE categories. The proportions used to distribute overseas tourist expenditure in New Zealand amongst HCE categories are updated infrequently. Electronic card transaction (ECT) data for foreign issued cards will  be used to update travel credit proportions for the following categories:
- accommodation services
- international air passenger services
- restaurant meals and ready to eat food
- non-alcoholic beverages.
Tourist spending in New Zealand (used in the calculation of HCE) is normally estimated using data collected via the international visitors survey (IVS). IVS data collection has ceased as a result of border closures introduced from March 2020. A new method for measuring international tourist expenditure has been developed to fill this gap. See Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter for further information about this new methodology.
Digital services imports will be included in the ‘Imports and household consumption expenditure (HCE) components of the expenditure measure of GDP. Within HCE, digital services will be in the Recreation and Culture group. Imports and HCE will revise upwards by equal amounts. As HCE has a positive contribution to GDP and imports have a negative contribution, the inclusion of digital services imports will have no net impact on total GDP. This change has been incorporated into GDP as part of our usual annual revisions from the Balance of Payments. See International trade: June 2020 – data sources and methods .

### Gross fixed capital formation
The following sub-component of gross fixed capital formation will depart from usual approaches:
- The local government component of other construction uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In March 2020 quarter an adjustment was made to account for the days in alert level 4 lockdown. In June 2020 quarter, BDC sales vales data will be used.
- Land improvements usually is interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In June 2020 quarter we used low level movements from other construction to represent this component.

#### Exports of travel services
Tourist spending in New Zealand is normally estimated using data collected via the international visitors survey (IVS). IVS data collection has ceased as a result of border closures introduced from March 2020. A new method for measuring international tourist expenditure has been developed to fill this gap. See Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter for further information about this new methodology.

#### Exports of education services
For education export services, we estimate the tuition fees paid by overseas students from Export Education Levy (EEL) data compiled by the Ministry of Education (MoE).
For the March and June 2020 quarters, we used updated 2019 Education Export Levy data from MoE and adjusted it using updated student visa numbers from Immigration New Zealand. See International trade June 2020 quarter data sources and methods .
